question:
Bonding in which of the following diatomic molecule(s) become(s) stronger, on the basis of MO Theory, by removal of an electron?

(A) NO

(B) N2

(C) O2

(D) C2

(E) B2

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
solution: In all these molecules O2​ and NO, is stabilized by removal of electrons because it is having electrons in the anti-bonding orbital. And thus removal of electron from the anti bonding orbital, increases the bond order of O2 and NO​. And thus is becomes more stable after removal of electrons. Bond order of O2+​is 2.5 and it is more than that of O2​ which is having bond order as 2. Similarly Bond order of NO is 2.5 and that of NO+ is 3.

question: Number of electron deficient molecules among the following <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>PH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>CCl</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mi>LiH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is
solution:
A molecule is said to be electron deficient because there are not enough valence electrons to form the expected number of covalent bonds. For example, in B2H6, there are only 12 electrons, three from each boron atoms and six from hydrogen, while ethane possesses 14 such electrons.

Also in BCl3, B has six electrons in its outermost orbit. So, the number of electron deficient molecules are 2.

q: The eutrophication of water body results in
question: The eutrophication of water body results in
solution: The addition of phosphates in water enhances algae growth. Such profuse growth of algae, covers the water surface and reduces the oxygen concentration in water. This leads to anaerobic conditions, commonly with accumulation of abnoxious decay and animal death. Thus, bloom-infested water inhibits the growth of other living organisms in the water body. This process in which nutrient enriched water bodies support a dense plant population, which kills animal life by depriving it of oxygen and results in subsequent loss of biodiversity is known as Eutrophication.

solution:
Extraction of gold is carried out by leaching process.

Sodium cyanide (NaCN) has been used as leaching reagent for gold.

Metal is converted into the soluble cyanide complex on reaction with sodium cyanide.

4Au + 8CN-+2H2O+O2 → 4[Au(CN)2]-X+4OH-

Silver is later recovered by displacement with Zn.

2AuCN2- + Zn →DisplacementZnCN42-Y + 2Au

X and Y are  [Au(CN)2]- and  [Zn(CN)4]2-, respectively.

q: Incorrect statement for Tyndall effect is
question: Incorrect statement for Tyndall effect is
options: The refractive indices of the dispersed phase and the dispersion medium differ greatly in magnitude.
The diameter of the dispersed particles is much smaller than the wavelength of the light used.
It is used to distinguish a true solution from a colloidal solution.
During projection of movies in the cinemas hall, Tyndall effect is noticed.
solution:
Tyndall effect is observed only when the following two conditions are satisfied.

(i) The diameter of the dispersed particles is not much smaller than the wavelength of the light used; and

(ii) The refractive indices of the dispersed phase and the dispersion medium differ greatly in magnitude.

This is a characteristic property of the colloid solutions.

=C1+2C2·3x2+3·5·C3x4+…+10·19C10x18

putting x=1

1029+18·28=C1+3·2·C2+5·3·C3+….+19·10·C10

C1+3·2·C2+……+19·10·C10

=10·29·10=100·29

Now 1+x10=C0+C1x+C2x2+……+C10x10

Integrating, we get

1+x111101=C0x+C1x22+C2x33+……+C10x111101

C0+C12+C23+….+C910+C1011=211-111

C0+C12+C23+….+C910=211-211

Now, 100·29=α·2112β-1211-211

⇒α=275, β=11⇒α+β=286

solution:

L1=4x-3y+K1=0; L2=4x-3y+K2=0

Here line L1 and L2 are parallel.

So,

Now point A-1,2 will satisfy L1 so, -4-3×2+K2=0 ⇒ K2=10

Also point B3,-6 will satisfy L2=4x-3y+K2

So 4×3-3×-6+K2=0  ⇒K2=-30

Now distance between the parallel line will be the diameter

Diameter =K1-K242+32

=10+305=8

So radius 82=4

Now mid-point of AB will give us centre of circle by symmetry, so by midpoint formula in AB we get centre 1,-2,

Now equation of circle will be x-12+y+22=42

solution:
Let the roots of 2x2-rx+p=0 are x1, x2 and roots of y2-sy-q=0 are y1, y2

So, x1+x2=r2, x1x2=p2, y1+y2=s, y1y2=-q

Equation of the circle with PQ as diameter will be 

x-x1x-x2+y-y1y-y2=0 

i.e. 2x2+y2-rx-2sy+p-2q=0 

On comparing with the given equation r=11,s=7

p-2q=-22

∴ 2r+s-2q+p=22+7-22=7

question: If wattless current flows in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AC</mi></math> circuit, then the circuit is :
solution: The current in an AC circuit containing only either a capacitor or an inductor is said to be wattless as the average power is zero. As wattless current has phase difference of ϕ=90° with voltage, therefore power factor cosϕ will be zero.

options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> deflection method cannot be used for determining the resistance of the galvanometer.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> deflection method can be used and in this case the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math> equals to twice the value of shunt resistance(s).

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> deflection method can be used and in this case, the G equals to three times the value of shunt resistance(s).

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> deflection method can be used and in this case the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math> value equals to the shunt resistance(s)
solution:
If a large resistance R is connected with a battery, then current will be approximately εR, and t will remain almost constant.

In 1st  case, only G is connected ⇒ig=i

In 2nd  case,

igR0=I-igS⇒ig2=SRg+Si

If ig2=13ig,⇒SRg+Si=13i

Rg=2S

If the current increases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the energy developed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.

If the current increases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the energy developed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
Energy produced is given by H=i2Rt

When i=2 A, t=15 s, energy is H=300=22×R×15

Resistance is R=5 Ω

When i=3 A, t=10 s.

Energy is H'=32×5×10=450 J

(Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow><mi>c</mi></mfrac></mfenced><msup><mi>NC</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the intensity of the wave if it is propagating along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis in the free space. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Given, E=56.5sinωt-xc N C-1

Comparing it with the general equation of electromagnetic wave, 

E=E0sin ωt-kx

We have, E0=56.5 N C-1

So, the intensity will be  I=12ε0E02c

=12×8.85×10-12×56.52×3×108

=4.24  W m-2

q: In the figure, a very large plane sheet of positive charge is shown. P1 and P2 are two points at distance l and 2l from the charge distribution. If σ is the surface charge density, then the magnitude of electric fields E1 and E2 at P1 and P2 respectively are
question: In the figure, a very large plane sheet of positive charge is shown. P1 and P2 are two points at distance l and 2l from the charge distribution. If σ is the surface charge density, then the magnitude of electric fields E1 and E2 at P1 and P2 respectively are
solution:
The electric field due to a large uniformly charged non-conducting sheet is given by, E=σ2εo. It is independent of the distance from the sheet. Therefore,

E1=E2=σ2εo

Here temperature is same.

Thus, vrmsvmost probable=3RT322RT32=32

Hence, vrms=32vp

q: A uniform chain of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> length is placed on a table such that a part of its length is hanging over the edge of the table. The system is at rest. The co-efficient of static friction between the chain and the surface of the table is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, the maximum length of the chain hanging from the table is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: A uniform chain of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> length is placed on a table such that a part of its length is hanging over the edge of the table. The system is at rest. The co-efficient of static friction between the chain and the surface of the table is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, the maximum length of the chain hanging from the table is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Let, λ=mass per unit length of the chain and length of mass hanging through the table is x.

Normal force applied by the table on the chain will be, N=l-xλg.

Tension developed in the chain will balance the gravitational force acting in the downward direction, T=λxg.

Maximum limiting force possible for the part of the table, fmax=μN=μl-xλg

For equilibrium,

fmax=T,

⇒μl-xλg=λxg⇒12×6-x=x ⇒x=2 m
